retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 main treatments for generalized anxiety disorder are cognitive behavioral therapy and medications. Patients may benefit most from a combination of the two. It may take some trial and error to discover which treatments work best. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Behavior Therapy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety: CBT often focuses on replacing negative automatic thoughts that can occur in generalized anxiety disorder and may be used alone or in combination with medications such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, which are typically first-line as benzodiazepines have a greater risk of adverse outcomes. In treating panic disorder, CBT may include desensitization to triggers that provoke anxiety; it is important to note, however, that a potential adverse effect of this technique is a temporary mild increase in anxiety. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Illness Anxiety Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Psychotherapy is the first-line treatment for IAD. [1] Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is a type of psychotherapy that focuses on treating the patient's dysfunctional maladaptive cognitive beliefs by behavioral modification strategies. It may address the patient's habits of excessive body checking for signs of illness. CBT also includes education about normal somatic sensations and their normal variations. [1] Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy, group therapies, and acceptance and commitment therapy may also be instituted.

retrieved_docs.title: Separation Anxiety Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
retrieved_docs.content: CBT is considered the first-line treatment for SAD and is an optimal choice for its efficacy and low risk of adverse effects. CBT should include techniques involving psychoeducation, changing maladaptive thought patterns, and gradual exposure to anxiety-provoking situations. Effective CBT typically requires 10 to 15 outpatient sessions (60-90 minutes each) to practice newly acquired skills at home. The treatment regime may be shortened or prolonged depending on the severity of symptoms and comorbid factors. [41] Notably, up to 44% of the pediatric population treated with CBT for anxiety disorders do not improve. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who do not respond to cognitive behavioral therapy may be treated with medications. Some patients with severe symptoms are treated with both initially. Several types of medications are used to treat generalized anxiety disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: The two main treatments for generalized anxiety disorder are cognitive behavior therapy and medications. Patients may benefit most from a combination of the two. It may take some trial and error to discover which treatments work best. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of generalized anxiety disorder. Apply the Generalized Anxiety Disorder 7-Item Questionnaire in the evaluation of generalized anxiety disorder. Implement cognitive-behavioral therapy in the management of patients with a generalized anxiety disorder. Collaborate and communicate among the interprofessional team to enhance care delivery for patients affected by a generalized anxiety disorder. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Behavior Therapy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the 1960s, Aaron Beck developed cognitive behavior therapy (CBT) or cognitive therapy. Since then, it has been extensively researched and found to be effective in a large number of outcome studies for some psychiatric disorders, including depression, anxiety disorders, eating disorders, substance abuse, and personality disorders. It also has been demonstrated to be effective as an adjunctive treatment to medication for serious mental disorders such as bipolar disorder and schizophrenia. CBT has been adapted and studied for children, adolescents, adults, couples, and families. Its efficacy also has been established in the treatment of non-psychiatric disorders such as irritable bowel syndrome, chronic fatigue syndrome, fibromyalgia, insomnia, migraines, and other chronic pain conditions. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (Nursing) -- Medical Management -- Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: This includes psychoeducation, changing maladaptive thoughts patterns, and gradual exposure to anxiety-provoking situations.

retrieved_docs.title: Person-Centered Therapy (Rogerian Therapy)  -- Clinical Significance -- Anxiety
retrieved_docs.content: Non-directive psychotherapy may be comparable to CBT and other forms of psychotherapy in treating generalized anxiety disorder in older adults. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Social Anxiety Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There is a large amount of evidence supporting the efficacy of medications and cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) in SAD. [15] According to meta-analysis, SAD responds well to treatment with individual CBT and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRI). Additionally, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) have a greater effect on outcomes than placebo. The SSRIs sertraline and paroxetine, as well as the SNRI venlafaxine, have been approved by the FDA. Comparing different psychotherapies, SAD responded better to CBT than psychodynamic therapy and other psychological therapies. The beta-blocker propranolol, as well as benzodiazepines, are also used in the treatment of SAD. Propranolol has the advantage of being used on an as-needed basis without the risk of developing dependence and tolerance, as exists with benzodiazepines. There is no evidence that combining pharmacological and psychological interventions is more efficacious than monotherapy. [16] A comparison of pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy trials suggests medication has faster effects, but CBT has longer-lasting effects. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anxiety -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Psychotherapy: One of the most effective forms of psychotherapy is cognitive-behavioral therapy. It is a structured, goal-oriented, and didactic form of therapy that focuses on helping individuals identify and modify characteristic maladaptive thinking patterns and beliefs that trigger and maintain symptoms. This form of therapy focuses on building behavioral skills so that patients can behave and react more adaptively to anxiety-producing situations. Exposure therapy is utilized to move individuals towards facing the anxiety-provoking situations and stimuli which they typically avoid. This exposure results in a reduction in anxiety symptoms as they learn that their anxiety is causing them to experience false alarms and they do not need to fear the situation or stimuli and can cope effectively with such a situation.

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for patients with generalized anxiety disorder is guarded. Many patients are not compliant with medications because of cost and adverse effects. Relapses are common, and patients often search for physicians who comply with their needs. Because of the lack of conventional medicine to cure the disorder, many opt for alternative therapies without much success. Overall, the quality of life of these patients is poor.

retrieved_docs.title: Anxiety -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Acute anxiety may require treatment with a benzodiazepine. Chronic anxiety treatment consists of psychotherapy, pharmacotherapy, or a combination of both.

retrieved_docs.title: Anxiety -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: SNRIs (venlafaxine and duloxetine) are considered as effective as SSRIs and also are considered first-line treatment, particularly for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1634
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified as a specific type of anxiety disorders and is defined by particular diagnostic criteria that guide its identification and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Psychological disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad category of mental health conditions that encompasses more specific disorders such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation‑anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال), each of which is a narrower subtype within the overall class of psychological disorders. Both the general category of psychological disorder and its child conditions—depression and separation‑anxiety disorder—are identified as targets for prevention and control measures, indicating that strategies aimed at preventing onset, reducing severity, and managing symptoms are applicable across the spectrum of these mental health conditions. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of applying preventive and control interventions at both the overarching disorder level and the individual disorder level to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1576
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders comprise a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific subtypes. One such subtype, Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, is classified under the related category Anxiety disorder due to known physiological condition and is defined by explicit diagnostic criteria that are incorporated into the overall diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. Another distinct entity, Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere, is recognized as a child category within the anxiety disorders hierarchy. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and the associated diagnostic criteria is essential for accurate identification and appropriate management of patients whose anxiety symptoms are linked to underlying medical or physiological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Gabapentin, an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain medication, may be employed in the therapeutic management of رُهاب (phobia). Although primarily indicated for seizures and neuropathic pain, gabapentin is sometimes used off‑label to alleviate anxiety‑related symptoms associated with specific phobic disorders, providing clinicians with an additional pharmacologic option for patients who experience heightened fear responses. This potential application underscores the broader utility of gabapentin in addressing certain anxiety conditions beyond its conventional uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders serve as a classification for حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic conditions. This relationship indicates that brief, organic‑based psychotic episodes are considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders, highlighting an overlap between anxiety symptomatology and short‑lasting psychotic states. Recognizing that حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة fall under anxiety disorders can help clinicians appropriately assess and manage patients who present with transient psychotic features in the context of anxiety‑related pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor used to treat depression and anxiety disorders, may have its therapeutic outcomes influenced by تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use or smoking). Smoking can alter the metabolism of citalopram, potentially affecting its efficacy and side‑effect profile. Therefore, clinicians should assess a patient’s smoking status when initiating or adjusting citalopram therapy to ensure optimal treatment response and safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental disorders constitute a broad group of conditions that affect neurodevelopmental processes. Within this overarching category, other psychiatric disorders are regarded as a subset, indicating that these psychiatric conditions share developmental origins or characteristics. Moreover, other psychiatric disorders are also classified as a form of رُهاب (phobia), reflecting that some of these conditions present primarily as intense, irrational fears. This dual classification underscores the overlap between developmental psychopathology and specific anxiety‑related disorders, highlighting the need for clinicians to consider developmental factors when diagnosing and managing phobic psychiatric presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2289
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder caused by drug is classified as اضطراب القلق بسبب حالة طبية عامة, meaning it is a type of anxiety disorder that arises secondary to a general medical condition, specifically the pharmacologic effects of a medication. This relationship highlights that drug‑induced anxiety is considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders linked to underlying medical illnesses, emphasizing the importance of recognizing medication side effects as a distinct etiological factor in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) and nonspecific chest pain are clinically associated, indicating that patients experiencing anxiety may also present with nonspecific chest pain and vice versa. Pharmacotherapy is recognized as an appropriate and permitted treatment option for both anxiety and nonspecific chest pain, highlighting the relevance of medication management in addressing these interrelated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التعب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that feelings of anxiety are integral to the clinical presentation of fatigue. In addition, dietotherapy is identified as an allowed qualifier for both anxiety (القلق) and fatigue (التعب), meaning that nutritional or dietary interventions are recognized as permissible therapeutic options for managing these conditions. This interconnection highlights the relevance of considering both psychological symptoms and dietary strategies in the comprehensive care of patients experiencing fatigue and anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor prescribed for depressive and anxiety disorders. Alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) is a substance‑use disorder that can interfere with the effectiveness and safety of citalopram, rather than serve as a treatment for it. Clinicians should be aware that patients with alcohol dependence may require careful monitoring, dose adjustments, or alternative therapeutic strategies when using citalopram.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1357
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified within anxiety disorders and adheres to the diagnostic criteria established for anxiety disorders. Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is also situated under the anxiety disorders category, identified as a subcategory (has parent) of anxiety disorders. Both entities illustrate how specific mental health conditions are organized under the broader anxiety disorders classification, emphasizing the role of diagnostic criteria and hierarchical relationships in accurate diagnosis and patient care.
